title: Acid-Base Balance MCQ – Chapter 40
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: In acute (noncompensated) respiratory acidosis, what happens to pH and pCO2?

options.B: pH markedly increased, pCO2 markedly decreased
options.C: pH markedly decreased, pCO2 markedly decreased
options.D: pH markedly decreased, pCO2 markedly increased

questions.explanation: Page 294, Table 40.2: In acute respiratory acidosis (noncompensated), pH shows marked decrease (↓↓) and pCO2 shows marked increase (↑↑↑).

questions.question: In chronic (compensated) respiratory acidosis, what is the expected HCO3 level?
questions.explanation: Page 294, Table 40.2: In chronic compensated respiratory acidosis, HCO3 shows moderate increase (↑↑) as renal compensation occurs.

questions.question: What is the pH finding in acute (noncompensated) respiratory alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Page 294, Table 40.2: In acute respiratory alkalosis (noncompensated), pH shows moderate increase (↑↑).

questions.question: In chronic compensated respiratory alkalosis, what is the pH?
questions.explanation: Page 294, Table 40.2: In chronic compensated respiratory alkalosis, pH is mildly increased (↑) or normal.

questions.question: In acute metabolic acidosis (noncompensated), what is the pCO2 finding?
questions.explanation: Page 294, Table 40.2: In acute metabolic acidosis (noncompensated), pCO2 shows mild decrease (↓) as early respiratory compensation begins.

questions.question: Metabolic acidosis is defined as a decrease in pH below:
questions.explanation: Page 294: Metabolic acidosis is defined as decrease in pH <7.35.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of metabolic acidosis?
questions.explanation: Page 294: Causes of metabolic acidosis include renal failure, circulatory failure (shock) leading to accumulation of lactic acid, hepatic failure, diarrhea with loss of bicarbonate, and cyanide poisoning. Diuretics are a cause of metabolic alkalosis, not acidosis.

questions.question: The normal anion gap is:
questions.explanation: Page 294: Normal anion gap = 8-16 mM/L (average 12 mM/L).

questions.question: The formula for anion gap is:
questions.explanation: Page 294: Anion gap = Sodium concentration – (Chloride + Bicarbonate concentration).

questions.question: Anion gap is mainly constituted by:
questions.explanation: Page 294: Anion gap is mainly constituted by plasma albumin. It is also called unmeasured anion concentration, normally constituted by sulfates, phosphates, organic acids and plasma proteins.

questions.question: According to Flow chart 40.1, if pH is decreased (<7.35) and pCO2 is increased with increased HCO3, the diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Page 294, Flow chart 40.1: pH decreased + increased pCO2 + increased HCO3 = Respiratory acidosis.

questions.question: According to Flow chart 40.1, if pH is decreased (<7.35), pCO2 is normal, and HCO3 is decreased, the diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Page 294, Flow chart 40.1: pH decreased + normal pCO2 + decreased HCO3 = Metabolic acidosis.

questions.question: According to Flow chart 40.1, if pH >7.45, pCO2 is decreased and HCO3 is decreased, the diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Page 294, Flow chart 40.1: pH increased (>7.45) + decreased pCO2 + decreased HCO3 = Respiratory alkalosis.

questions.question: According to Flow chart 40.1, if pH is normal (7.35-7.45), pCO2 is increased, and HCO3 is increased, the diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Page 294, Flow chart 40.1: Normal pH + increased pCO2 + increased HCO3 = Respiratory acidosis + Metabolic alkalosis (mixed disorder with full compensation).

questions.question: Which of the following is a cause of respiratory alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Page 294: Causes of respiratory alkalosis include Iatrogenic, Pregnancy, Salicylate poisoning, Hypoxia, and CNS trauma.

questions.question: Treatment of respiratory alkalosis includes:

options.C: Adjustment of ventilator setting (decrease frequency) and increasing rebreathing

questions.explanation: Page 294: Treatment of respiratory alkalosis: Adjustment of ventilator setting (decrease the frequency) and increasing the rebreathing (i.e. exhaled gases containing CO2).

questions.question: In COPD patients, respiratory acidosis may coexist with metabolic acidosis due to:

options.A: Decreased cardiac output (cor pulmonale) and renal flow

questions.explanation: Page 294: Respiratory acidosis in COPD patients may be associated with metabolic acidosis due to decreased cardiac output (cor pulmonale) and renal flow.

questions.question: Treatment of metabolic alkalosis includes IV infusion of:
questions.explanation: Page 294: Treatment of metabolic alkalosis: Treat the underlying cause, IV infusion of ammonium chloride or 0.1N hydrochloric acid (not more than 0.2 mEq/kg/hr).

questions.question: The maximum rate of 0.1N HCl infusion for treating metabolic alkalosis should not exceed:
questions.explanation: Page 294: IV infusion of 0.1N hydrochloric acid should not be more than 0.2 mEq/kg/hr.

questions.question: Which of the following is a cause of metabolic alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Page 294: Causes of metabolic alkalosis include Diuretics, Hypovolemia, Diarrhea with loss of chloride, and Iatrogenic.

questions.question: In acute metabolic alkalosis (noncompensated), pCO2 shows:
questions.explanation: Page 294, Table 40.2: In acute metabolic alkalosis (noncompensated), pCO2 shows moderate increase (↑↑) as respiratory compensation (hypoventilation) occurs.

questions.question: According to Flow chart 40.1, if pH is decreased (<7.35), pCO2 is increased, and HCO3 is decreased or normal, the diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Page 294, Flow chart 40.1: pH decreased + increased pCO2 + decreased/normal HCO3 = Respiratory acidosis + Metabolic acidosis (mixed disorder).

questions.question: Circulatory failure (shock) leading to accumulation of which acid causes metabolic acidosis?
questions.explanation: Page 294: Causes of metabolic acidosis include circulatory failure (shock) leading to accumulation of lactic acid.

questions.question: According to Flow chart 40.1, if pH is normal (7.35-7.45), pCO2 is decreased, and HCO3 is decreased, the likely diagnoses include:

options.A: Chronic metabolic acidosis (renal disease) and chronic respiratory alkalosis (pulmonary disease)

questions.explanation: Page 294, Flow chart 40.1: Normal pH + decreased pCO2 + decreased HCO3 = Chronic metabolic acidosis (due to renal disease) and chronic respiratory alkalosis due to pulmonary disease.

questions.question: In acute metabolic acidosis (noncompensated), HCO3 shows:
questions.explanation: Page 294, Table 40.2: In acute metabolic acidosis (noncompensated), HCO3 shows marked decrease (↓↓↓).
